title: From Chaos to Synchrony in Recurrent Excitatory-Inhibitory Networks with Target-Specific Inhibition
authors: Carles Martorell, Rubén Calvo, Alessia Annibale, Miguel A. Muñoz
abstract: Biological neural networks can operate in qualitatively distinct dynamical regimes, and transitions between these regimes are thought to underlie changes in computation and behavior. The seminal work of Sompolinsky, Crisanti, and Sommers (SCS) showed that random recurrent networks undergo a transition from quiescence to asynchronous chaos, establishing a paradigmatic link between random connectivity, dynamical instability, and internally generated fluctuations in neural circuits. Here, we extend this framework to two-population firing-rate networks with segregated excitatory and inhibitory neurons and target-specific inhibitory couplings that break excitation--inhibition balance. Using dynamical mean-field theory, we derive self-consistent equations for the macroscopic mean activities and autocorrelations, together with stability criteria distinguishing mean-driven and fluctuation-driven instabilities. We show that target-specific inhibition organizes the phase diagram into three qualitative classes: inhibition-dominated or strictly balanced networks display only quiescent activity and asynchronous chaos; excitation-dominated networks display persistent activity together with either synchronous chaos with non-vanishing mean activity or coherent oscillations, depending on the stability-matrix eigenvalues. Crucially, coherent oscillations do not coexist with chaotic fluctuations around the periodic mean trajectory; rather, their onset suppresses the chaotic component, reminiscent of input-induced suppression of chaos. These results generalize SCS theory to recurrent networks with explicit excitatory--inhibitory structure and identify target-specific inhibition as a key control parameter for large-scale neural dynamics.
summary: This paper extends the classic Sompolinsky-Crisanti-Sommers theory to E/I networks with structured inhibition. It demonstrates that tuning how inhibition targets different populations can fundamentally reorganize the network's phase diagram, even suppressing chaos in favor of synchronized oscillations.
main_problem: Understanding how target-specific inhibition in recurrent excitatory-inhibitory (E/I) networks regulates the emergence of macroscopic dynamical regimes like asynchronous chaos, persistent activity, and coherent oscillations.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct classes of phase diagrams based on inhibitory coupling, showing that target-specific inhibition can suppress chaotic fluctuations and drive the system into coherent oscillatory activity.
method: The authors use Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) and the Martin-Siggia-Rose-De Dominicis-Janssen formalism to derive self-consistent equations for macroscopic observables and perform linear stability analysis.
model_or_system: A two-population recurrent neural network consisting of segregated excitatory and inhibitory neurons with sparse, asymmetric Gaussian connectivity and target-specific inhibitory couplings.
key_observables: Mean activities, two-time autocorrelation functions, Largest Lyapunov Exponent (LLE), Kuramoto order parameter, and phase lag between populations.
important_parameters: Target-specific inhibition parameters (beta and delta), coupling strength (g), connectivity mean (J0) and variance (J2), and the fraction of excitatory neurons (f).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity), assumes a tanh activation function, and uses a sparse connectivity construction to preserve Dale's principle.
figures_summary: Figures show phase diagrams and heat maps of mean activity and autocorrelation, trajectories of E/I activity, eigenvalue spectra, and the Largest Lyapunov Exponent across parameter spaces.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the E/I network model, develops the DMFT framework for sparse networks, classifies phase diagrams based on inhibitory parameters, analyzes stability transitions (Type I and II), and validates results with numerical simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Nature of the leading eigenvalue of Nβ,δ —determining the type of resulting phase diagram— across the (β, δ) plane. The table on the right summarizes the classification of the leading eigenvalue into three distinct cases, denoted by S1, S2, and S3. The corresponding conditions associated to these cases split the (β, δ) parameter space into the three regions as shown in the left panel. In this panel, solid lines denote the analytical boundaries between these regimes, while the dashed line identifies the symmetric-inhibition condition, β = δ. Violet pentagons identify three representative parameter choices analyzed in Fig. 2.

Figure 2: Phase diagrams and representative trajectories for (β, δ) = (0.50, 0.50) (point A in Fig. 1; panels A–E), (β, δ) = (0.50, 0.25) (point B in Fig. 1; panels F–J), and (β, δ) = (1.50, 1.25) (point C in Fig. 1; panels K–O). Panels A,B,F,G, K, L show heat maps of the time-averaged mean inhibitory activity ˆMy and the equal-time autocorrelation ˆCy(0). Solid black lines are bifurcation curves from fixed-point stability [Eqs. (22,20)], while the dashed and solid gray lines are transitions of stationary chaotic/oscillatory states from Eqs. (28) and (29), respectively. Panels C–E (for (β, δ) = (0.50, 0.50)), H–J (for (β, δ) = (0.50, 0.25)) and M–O (for (β, δ) = (1.50, 1.25)) display...

Figure 3: Largest Lyapunov exponent across dynamical regimes. Heat map of the largest Lyapunov exponent (LLE) Λ for a network of size N = 1000 with (β, δ) = (0.5, 0.5) (panel A) and (β, δ) = (0.5, 0.25) (panel B) plotted as a function of the rescaled parameters ((J0/J)∗, (1/gJ)∗). Positive values of Λ identify chaotic regimes, while oscillatory activity is linked with a zero value. The LLE has been averaged over S = 100 different realizations. Bifurcation lines are depicted identically to those of Fig. 2.

Figure 4: Oscillatory regimes and transition routes to the COA phase. Panel A: Time-averaged Kuramoto parameter Ry of the inhibitory population as a function of the rescaled parameters ((J0/J)∗, (1/gJ)∗), indicating the degree of global phase synchronization. Panels B–D: Rep- resentative dynamical states corresponding to colored pentagons in panel A. These panels show the excitatory (red) and inhibitory (blue) activity for some neurons, xi(t) and yi(t), and the mean activities ˆMx,y(t) (thicker lines), together with the population-averaged power spectrum within the AC phase, at ((J0/J)∗, (1/gJ)∗) = (0.25, 0.25); and COA phase, at (1.25, 1.25) and (1, 0.25), respectively.
